message:
perf(ft-kernel-cpu): pairwise summation in sum/mean reductions

`sum_tensor_contiguous_f64` (and the f32 sibling, plus the two mean
helpers that compose them) used the naive `slice.iter().sum()` —
sequential left-to-right f64 accumulation with O(N · ε) error
accumulation. PyTorch uses pairwise (binary-tree) summation per
Higham 1993 for the same precision reason: at N = 10^6 the naive
sum can drift O(10^6 · ε) ≈ 2.2e-10 relative error while pairwise
stays at O(log(N) · ε) ≈ 4.4e-15, a six-orders-of-magnitude
improvement that becomes visible on any sufficiently-large tensor
reduction.

Implementation: new `pairwise_sum_f64` / `pairwise_sum_f32` private
helpers using divide-and-conquer down to a 128-element block (below
which sequential is already ULP-quality and the recursion overhead
hurts more than the precision gain). Inserted into all four
contiguous-sum / contiguous-mean entry points; `sum_dim_*`
intentionally left unchanged because non-contiguous strided
accumulation can't pairwise-fold without first gathering the slice
(separate optimization tracked for a follow-up).

Tests:
  * `sum_tensor_contiguous_pairwise_handles_block_boundary` —
    verifies the 128-element BLOCK split yields the same result as
    sequential for trivial inputs at sizes 127, 128, 129, 200, 256,
    257, 512, 1000 (catches any future bug where the recursion base
    case got swapped).
  * `sum_tensor_contiguous_pairwise_beats_naive_on_adversarial_input`
    — runs a 1M-element pattern and locks (a) an absolute drift
    bound the pairwise path achieves and (b) the floor that pairwise
    is no worse than naive (with a small slack for tree-alignment
    edge cases). The naive path *failed* the absolute bound on the
    pre-pairwise implementation.

Quality gates: 12-crate workspace test sweep — ft-kernel-cpu 305 +
ft-api 1090 + ft-conformance 101 + ft-autograd 314 + ft-nn 150 +
ft-optim 74 + ft-data 103 + ft-serialize 15 + ft-runtime 2 +
ft-core 70 + ft-dispatch 9 + ft-device — all green; clippy + ubs
clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
